---
title: "Final portfolio, reflection, and/or conference"
subtitle: "Bringing the term's work together"
toc: true
---

> **Pairs with:** Weeks 13–15. Due dates and submission link live in
> Blackboard.

## Goal

Assemble the small portfolio you've built across the term into a
**single, polished, well-organized collection**, and pair it with a
**short written reflection**. Depending on the term's format, you may
also have a brief **individual conference**, an **optional short
demo**, or submit a **recorded walkthrough**.

This is the course's capstone. There are no new technical skills to
learn — the work is **curation, polish, and reflection**.

## What's required vs. what's flexible

**Required for everyone:**

- A **portfolio** — a small, well-organized collection of the term's
  artifacts (see "What to include" below).
- A **final reflection** — a 1–2 page Quarto document.

**Format of the closing conversation — flexible**, announced in
Blackboard each term. Possible formats include:

- A **submitted portfolio** with no live component.
- A **short individual conference** (5–10 minutes, in person or over
  video).
- An **optional short demo** during the final-week meeting.
- A **recorded walkthrough** (screen recording with voiceover).

You will know which format applies to your section well before the
final week.

## Portfolio location — your choice

The portfolio may live as:

- a **local folder** on your computer,
- a **private repository** (GitHub, GitLab, etc.), or
- a **public repository**, if and only if you specifically choose to
  make your work public.

Public posting is encouraged when appropriate. **It is not required**
for assignment submission. Most students submit a zipped local
folder; a few choose a public repo. Either way, **Blackboard is the
submission inbox**.

## What to include

A complete portfolio contains:

| Component | What it is |
|-----------|-----------|
| **README** | A short index that says who you are, what's in the folder, and how to render anything. |
| **A selection of weekly homework** | 6–8 of your stronger weekly Quarto documents. You don't need every one. |
| **LaTeX Project** | The PDF and source. |
| **R Project** | The Quarto source and rendered PDF, plus any data. |
| **AI module artifacts** | Your AI debugging audit and your AI Use Reflection. |
| **(Optional) Bridge mini-report** | If you did HW 11, include it. |
| **Final reflection** | 1–2 pages — see below. |

## README expectations

A short Markdown file at the root of your portfolio. Something like:

```markdown
# math-software-portfolio — YOUR NAME

A small portfolio of mathematical software work from MATH 12362.

## Contents

- `hw01/` — first Quarto document, setup
- `hw02/` — math in LaTeX
- ...
- `latex-project/` — LaTeX paper replication
- `r-project/` — R data / simulation report
- `ai-module/` — debugging audit and reflection
- `reflection.pdf` — final reflection

## How to render

Each folder contains a `.qmd` (or `.tex`) source. From a folder, run
`quarto render <file>.qmd` (or `pdflatex` for `.tex` sources).
```

The README isn't graded for prose quality. It's graded for whether
**someone unfamiliar with your work can navigate the folder**.

## Final reflection

A 1–2 page Quarto document called `reflection.qmd` (rendered to PDF).

Address these four questions, briefly and honestly:

1. **What did you build?** A paragraph summarizing the portfolio.
2. **What did you learn about each pillar** (LaTeX, R, technical
   workflow, generative AI literacy) that you didn't know in Week 1?
3. **Where did AI help, and where did it hurt?** Concrete examples.
   This connects to the **verification** line in your AI Use Notes:
   what did you catch, and what almost slipped through?
4. **What would you do differently next term?** Tool/skill-wise and
   workflow-wise.

This is reflective writing, not a brag sheet. Honest self-assessment
is worth more than overclaiming. Include a short **AI Use Note** in
the reflection (Tool / Purpose / Verification).

## If your term includes a conference or optional demo

The format will be announced in Blackboard. General expectations if
this applies:

- **Conference** — a short 1-on-1 conversation about your portfolio.
  Be ready to walk me through one or two pieces, talk about what was
  hard, and answer questions about your verification choices.
- **Optional demo** — 5–10 minutes, live in the final-week meeting.
  Open with what the artifact is, show source + rendered output,
  highlight one technical thing you learned.
- **Recorded walkthrough** — 5–8 minutes, screen recording with
  voice. Same structure as a live demo.

None of these is universally required. Default to "portfolio +
reflection" unless your section is told otherwise.

## Grading

This piece is evaluated across the course's four provisional
categories, with weight on **technical workflow** (organization,
reproducibility, README clarity), **mathematical writing** (the
reflection itself), and **generative AI literacy** (the AI Use Note
and the AI portion of your reflection). Specific category weights for
the term are announced in Blackboard.

## AI use

You're welcome to use AI to help organize, polish, or write portions
of the reflection. The same rules apply: include an **AI Use Note**
in the reflection (Tool / Purpose / Verification), and don't submit
prose you can't explain.
